universalLoop.title: Universal Self-Improvement Loop
universalLoop.subtitle: A config-driven audit engine and self-improvement loop that works in any project, any stack, any agent client.
universalLoop.overviewDesc: This page proves that the self-improvement loop works in any project, any stack, any agent client.
universalLoop.architectureDesc: Two components power the loop: universal-audit.sh — a config-driven audit engine that scans code against project-specific rules — and the self-improvement skill — an orchestrator that interprets audit results and drives the fix pipeline.
universalLoop.flow1: init-agents — bootstraps the project with audit config, scripts, and skills
universalLoop.flow2: scaffolding — creates PATTERNS.md, ANTI-PATTERNS.md, ADR skeletons
universalLoop.flow3: audit detects — universal-audit.sh scans codebase and emits structured JSON
universalLoop.flow4: skill diagnoses — self-improvement skill matches findings against patterns
universalLoop.flow5: agent proposes — agent presents a fix plan with rationale
universalLoop.flow6: human approves — user reviews and approves or rejects the proposal
universalLoop.flow7: fix → verify → commit — approved fix is applied, re-audited, and committed
universalLoop.keyFilesDesc: The init-agents command creates 7 artifacts that form the loop:
universalLoop.file1Desc: Configuration for the audit engine: excludes, severity levels, terminology rules
universalLoop.file2Desc: The audit script that runs checks and outputs structured JSON
universalLoop.file3Desc: Skill directory containing the orchestrator and pattern definitions
universalLoop.file4Desc: Catalog of recognized improvement patterns with severity and recommended actions
universalLoop.file5Desc: Catalog of anti-patterns to detect and avoid
universalLoop.file6Desc: Architecture Decision Records directory for documenting audit findings
universalLoop.file7Desc: Automated ADR generator for documenting applied fixes
universalLoop.stackDesc: The loop auto-detects the project's language and configures exclusion rules accordingly:
universalLoop.stackNode: Node.js — detects package.json, excludes node_modules/, uses npm test
universalLoop.stackPython: Python — detects pyproject.toml or requirements.txt, excludes __pycache__/
universalLoop.stackConfigDesc: The auto-detected configuration is written to .audit-config.json and can be edited manually.
universalLoop.agentClientDesc: The loop uses a detect_target mechanism to adapt its output to the active agent:
universalLoop.client1Format: Standard skill invocation via skill() tool
universalLoop.crossPlatformDesc: On Unix systems, the init command creates symlinks for shared scripts. On Windows (Git Bash), it falls back to copies. The loop works on any operating system.
universalLoop.quickStartDesc: Initialize the loop in any project with a single command, then run the audit and improvement cycle:

hero.title: Documentation
hero.subtitle: Everything you need to use Another Agent Skills.

overview.title: Overview
overview.welcome: Another Agent Skills is a framework of 57 composable skills that turn AI coding agents into disciplined senior engineers.
overview.gettingStartedDesc: Installation, setup, and first project walkthrough.
overview.lifecycleDesc: The 6-phase development flow: Define, Plan, Build, Verify, Review, Ship.
overview.skillsDesc: Browse all 57 skills with filtering by category and lifecycle phase.
overview.enforcementDesc: 6-component harness architecture: instructions, tools, sandboxes, orchestration, guardrails, observability.
overview.philosophyDesc: SOUL.md principles, honest limitations, and what this framework avoids.
overview.agentsDesc: Compatible agents: OpenCode, Claude Code, Cursor, Devin Desktop, and more.
overview.evalDesc: Eval system: format spec, skill tiers, adding evals, running tests, and coverage checklist.
overview.quickstartDesc: First session walkthrough, how skills activate, common scenarios, and tips.
overview.whatsNewDesc1: Every skill now declares artifact, format, location, and quality criteria — no more guessing what a skill produces.
overview.whatsNewDesc2: Check 16 (missing contracts) eliminated: 37→0. Word count advisories resolved: 4→0. Pre-flight gate enforces .gitignore + .env.example.
overview.whatsNewDesc3: WORKFLOW-SCENARIOS.md: 6 practical browser testing scenarios covering console errors, forms, responsive, performance, visual, keyboard.

gettingStarted.title: Getting Started
gettingStarted.prereqAgent: OpenCode recommended, or any compatible agent (Claude Code, Cursor, Devin Desktop, Kiro)
gettingStarted.newProjectDesc: Start from scratch in an empty directory:
gettingStarted.nextStepsSummary: After running, you'll see a "Next Steps" summary:
gettingStarted.existingProjectDesc: If you already have code and want to add skill-driven enforcement:
gettingStarted.existingStep4: Detects your stack and creates STACK_CONFIG.md if missing
gettingStarted.stackDetectionDesc: init-agents automatically detects your project stack from lockfiles and config files:
gettingStarted.stackCreates: It creates STACK_CONFIG.md with your actual commands (test, lint, build, dev). All skills read from this file.
gettingStarted.openCodeDesc: No extra setup. Skills load automatically via the skill tool.
gettingStarted.claudeCodeDesc: Creates .claude-plugin/agent-discipline/ with hooks. To also load SOUL.md and AGENTS.md rules, copy the key principles into your CLAUDE.md.
gettingStarted.devinDesktopDesc: Creates .kiro/hooks/agent-discipline.json with pre-flight, commit approval, and edit guard hooks.
gettingStarted.anyGitAgentDesc: The git hooks work with any agent that uses git. Copy the hooks to your project:

lifecycle.title: The 6-Phase Lifecycle
lifecycle.subtitle: Every project follows the same disciplined flow. No shortcuts. No skipped steps.
lifecycle.phase1Goal: Write the spec before any code.
lifecycle.phase3Goal: Implement one slice at a time.
lifecycle.phase4Goal: Run tests. Check builds. Verify behavior.
lifecycle.phase4ToolGapDesc: If tools cannot reach the world, report "ship status unknown." Don't fake a win. The absence of evidence is not evidence of absence.
lifecycle.phase5DesignDesc: If UI work, run the 9-skill design review pipeline: critique → audit → clarify → hard → polish → typeset → adapt → optimize → delight
lifecycle.purposeDesc: The lifecycle weights phases by session purpose:
lifecycle.phase1Desc: Write the spec before any code. Interview requirements. Lock scope.
lifecycle.phase2Desc: Break work into atomic tasks. Each task must be independently testable.
lifecycle.phase3Desc: Implement one slice at a time. Test each slice before expanding.
lifecycle.phase4Desc: Run tests. Check builds. Verify behavior.

skills.title: Skills Catalog
skills.subtitle: 57 composable skills. Each one handles a specific engineering discipline.
skills.engineering_fundamentals: Universal engineering philosophy. Discovery, contracts, anti-slop, quality gates.
skills.context_engineering: Feed agents the right information at the right time.
skills.frontend_web: Production-grade web interfaces with anti-AI-slop rules.
skills.frontend_mobile: Native mobile apps with platform compliance.
skills.frontend_desktop: Cross-platform desktop apps with native OS integration.
skills.industrial_brutalist_ui: Swiss typographic print with military terminal aesthetics.
skills.minimalist_ui: Editorial product UI inspired by Notion and Linear.
skills.soft_premium_ui: Polished, calm, expensive UI with premium fonts.
skills.redesign_skill: Systematic approach to improving existing codebases.
skills.backend_api_mastery: Production APIs: protocol, DB, auth, testing, docs.
skills.cli_tools: Arg parsing, exit codes, colors, progress bars.
skills.doubt_driven_development: Adversarial fresh-context review of non-trivial decisions.
skills.interview_me: Extract what the user actually wants.
skills.idea_refine: Refine vague ideas into sharp concepts.
skills.code_review_and_quality: Five-axis review with severity labels and TOOL_GAP.
skills.test_driven_development: Failing test first, then make it pass.
skills.performance_optimization: Measure first, optimize only what matters.
skills.documentation_and_adrs: Document the why, not just the what.
skills.hard_skill: Deterministic mechanical fixes for P0/P1 issues.
skills.polish_skill: Design detail fixes: spacing, alignment, consistency.
skills.optimize_skill: Performance fixes: bundle, lazy loading, images.
skills.ci_cd_and_automation: Automated quality gates on every change.
skills.project_metrics: Build pass rate, rework, coverage logging.

enforcement.title: The Harness
enforcement.subtitle: 6 components. 14 gates. Zero shortcuts. Agent = Model + Harness.
enforcement.level1Desc: Agent remembers rules. Load-bearing for simple tasks.
enforcement.level2Desc: Agent presents DECISION POINT before every mutation.
enforcement.level3Desc: commit-msg hook v4 runs a single TDD gate — staged code files must have matching test files. Name-pairing + new-test enforcement.
enforcement.level4Desc: Agent cannot write commit approval without writing a commit manifest first.
enforcement.honestDesc: These gates create friction, not guarantees. The human must stay alert.
enforcement.gatesDesc: The pre-commit hook runs 14 checks before every commit:
enforcement.gate1: Branch check — not on detached HEAD
enforcement.gate3: Remote sync — no unpulled remote changes
enforcement.gate4: HTML integrity — staged HTML files have required structural markers
enforcement.gate5: Override escalation — 3 overrides on a branch triggers review
enforcement.gate6: Skill gate — skills were consulted before implementation
enforcement.gate7: Build verification — shell scripts have valid syntax
enforcement.gate8: Anti-slop — no generic AI-generated patterns detected
enforcement.gate9: Debug tracking — fix/debug commits tracked, escalates at 3
enforcement.gate10: SPEC enforcement — warn on new scripts without spec update
enforcement.gate11: Progress status validation — skill table must match file system
enforcement.gate12: Skill lint — validate skill structure on SKILL.md changes
enforcement.gate13: Eval gate — run evals, dashboard, and regression on changed skills
enforcement.gate14: Test Runner — runs tests/run-all.sh before every commit
enforcement.commitMsgDesc: The commit-msg hook runs a single TDD gate after the commit message is written:
enforcement.cmsgGate1: TDD gate — staged code files must have matching test files
enforcement.harnessDesc: A harness is the mechanical infrastructure that enforces agent behavior. This project implements 6 harness components that turn "please follow the rules" into "you literally cannot break them":
enforcement.harnessComp1: 1. Instructions & Rules — AGENTS.md, SOUL.md, STEERING-GUIDE.md, ANTI-PATTERNS.md, GLOSSARY.md. Who the agent is and what it must never do.
enforcement.harnessComp2: 2. Tools — 57 skills loaded on demand via skill gate. Task-specific capabilities, not generic prompts.
enforcement.harnessComp3: 3. Sandboxes & Execution — Terminal, git workspace, CI. Where the agent acts.
enforcement.harnessComp4: 4. Orchestration — skill-gate.sh, init-agents.sh, multi-agent patterns. When tools fire and how agents coordinate.
enforcement.harnessComp5: 5. Guardrails & Hooks — Pre-commit v11 (14 gates, Test Runner), commit-msg v4 (TDD enforcement), commit-approval.sh. Deterministic hooks at lifecycle points.
enforcement.harnessComp6: 6. Observability — project-metrics, HEALTH-CHECK.md, PROGRESS_STATUS.md, validate-skill-table.sh. Evidence that it's working or quietly drifting.
enforcement.incidentDesc: Four Rule 12 violations documented, each producing mechanical enforcement:
enforcement.incident1: INCIDENT_001: Agent committed without approval → Added visible manifest requirement
enforcement.incident2: INCIDENT_002: Agent bypassed manifest → Added time-window approval (replaced SHA256 tokens)
enforcement.incident3: INCIDENT_003: Agent generated tokens without real approval → Added mandatory manifest gate
enforcement.incident4: INCIDENT_004: Agent committed directly to main with test bypass → Added main branch protection + logged test bypass + commit-approval branch check
enforcement.scriptsDesc: Key scripts that enforce the workflow:
enforcement.script1Desc: Write commit approval (requires manifest, time-window checked)
enforcement.manifestDesc: The approval flow ensures changes are reviewed before committing:

philosophy.title: Philosophy
philosophy.subtitle: SOUL.md principles and what this framework avoids.
philosophy.principle1: Quality emerges from explicit contracts, not implicit trust.
philosophy.principle2: Rules that depend on memory fail. Rules that depend on visible blocks succeed.
philosophy.principle3: A hook that accepts any token is a suggestion. A hook that verifies integrity is a gate.
philosophy.principle4: Speed is a side effect of precision, not the primary goal.
philosophy.principle5: Context is finite. Evict before you drown.
philosophy.principle6: No, that's overcomplicated is more valuable than blind compliance.
philosophy.principle7: We learn from our own failures faster than competitors ship features.
philosophy.honestDesc: These gates create friction, not guarantees. The human must stay alert.
philosophy.principle1Desc: An agent that "knows" the rules will eventually break them. An agent that must present a DECISION BLOCK before every mutation cannot skip the decision. The difference is architectural, not behavioral.
philosophy.principle2Desc: Three incidents taught us this. Rule 12 violations happened not because the agent didn't know the rule, but because knowing isn't doing. The fix was not "remind the agent harder." The fix was mechanical enforcement.
philosophy.principle3Desc: Evolution of enforcement: v1 process rules, v2 visible manifest, v3 time-window approval, v4 manifest gate. Each level removed a failure mode.
philosophy.principle4Desc: The METR study found developers 19% slower with AI but feeling 20% faster. Speed without correctness is liability.
philosophy.principle5Desc: Our lazy-loading architecture (skills as ~250-line indexes, guides loaded on-demand) saves 50% of always-loaded tokens.
philosophy.principle6Desc: The Mayéutic Challenge: agents that push back, surface tradeoffs, and question scope creep.
philosophy.principle7Desc: Three Rule 12 violations in 48 hours produced three levels of enforcement. Each incident was documented, analyzed, and mechanically prevented.
philosophy.principle8Desc: When tools cannot reach the world, the honest answer is "ship status unknown," not "looks good to me."
philosophy.antiRationalDesc: Common rationalizations and why they are wrong:
philosophy.rational2Why: No. Skip OPTIONAL phases, not mandatory ones.
philosophy.rational3Why: Every commit is a separate decision.
philosophy.rational4: "It's faster to do it all at once."
philosophy.rational4Why: It feels faster until something breaks and you can't find which of 500 changed lines caused it.
philosophy.rational5Why: Confidence correlates poorly with correctness on novel problems.
philosophy.metrTitle: METR Study: Speed Is an Illusion
philosophy.metrDesc: The METR study (Becker et al., July 2025) found that when experienced developers used AI, they took 19% longer but estimated they saved 20% of the time. The perception of speed does not match reality. This is why this framework optimizes for getting it right, and speed follows.
philosophy.mayeuticDesc: Agents that push back, surface tradeoffs, and question scope creep. An agent that says "no" when justified prevents more damage than one that says "yes" to everything. Enforced via the task manifest system: before non-trivial tasks, the agent must write a manifest with files affected, edge cases, alternatives, and risks.
philosophy.value1c: A bad commit is faster to make than to fix
philosophy.value2c: One complete skill beats five stubs
philosophy.value3c: If a senior engineer says "overcomplicated," it is
philosophy.value4c: The agent suggests. The human decides.
philosophy.value5c: A feature without a gate is a liability
philosophy.value6c: Recap wastes tokens. Continuation preserves momentum.
philosophy.antiGoal1: Prioritizing speed over approval — every mutation requires explicit human consent
philosophy.antiGoal2: Shipping without mechanical enforcement — if a rule can be bypassed by forgetting, it is a suggestion
philosophy.antiGoal3: Making the agent dumber to save tokens — token optimization affects instruction tokens only
philosophy.antiGoal4: Adding quantity over quality — 31 curated skills beat 313 mediocre ones
philosophy.antiGoal5: Trusting the agent self-report — "tests pass" means nothing without running them
philosophy.antiGoal6: Abandoning the human in the loop — the agent suggests, the human decides
philosophy.machineWarning: Important: These principles apply to both the agent and the user. The agent is a machine with no inherent judgment — it follows patterns, not understanding. The user must stay alert, verify outputs, and make final decisions. Mechanical enforcement reduces risk but cannot eliminate it.
philosophy.machineWarningTitle: The Agent Is a Machine, Not a Colleague
philosophy.machineWarningBody: An AI agent follows patterns, not understanding. It generates plausible outputs without grasping consequences.
philosophy.machineWarningUser: The user must stay alert. Verify outputs. Make final decisions. Mechanical enforcement reduces risk but cannot eliminate it.
philosophy.machineWarningRef: This is not opinion — it is documented reality. The METR study found AI slowed experienced developers 19% while they estimated 20% savings (Becker et al., 2025, https://arxiv.org/abs/2507.09089). Multi-agent systems still require human oversight for critical decisions (Guo et al., 2024, https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.01680). Autonomous agents need human-in-the-loop frameworks to handle failures (Shapiro et al., 2023, https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.06775). Meaningful human oversight requires evaluative agency, not just presence (Zhu et al., 2026, https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43681-026-01147-7). Risk-based frameworks link AI risk level to oversight form (Kandikatla & Radeljic, 2025, https://arxiv.org/abs/2510.09090).
philosophy.ref1: Becker et al. (2025) — METR study: AI slowed experienced developers 19%
philosophy.ref2: Guo et al. (2024) — Multi-agent systems require human oversight
philosophy.ref3: Shapiro et al. (2023) — Autonomous agents need human-in-the-loop
philosophy.ref4: Zhu et al. (2026) — Meaningful human oversight requires evaluative agency
philosophy.ref5: Kandikatla & Radeljic (2025) — Risk-based framework for oversight

agents.title: Agent Compatibility
agents.subtitle: Git hooks work everywhere. Skill concepts load automatically for OpenCode.
agents.openCodeDesc: No extra setup. Skills load automatically via the skill tool. This is the recommended way to use Another Agent Skills.
agents.openCodeExample: Just run init-agents in any project. The agent detects your stack, loads matching skills, and enforces the lifecycle automatically.
agents.claudeCodeDesc: Copy rules into CLAUDE.md. Install adapter hooks. Skills load via CLAUDE.md reference.
agents.claudeCodeExample: bash install.sh --agent claude — creates .claude-plugin/ with hooks. Copy SOUL.md and AGENTS.md key principles into your CLAUDE.md.
agents.cursorDesc: Add rules to .cursorrules. Install adapter hooks. Skills load via .cursorrules reference.
agents.cursorExample: bash install.sh --agent cursor — creates .cursor-plugin/ with hooks. Append SOUL.md and AGENTS.md to .cursorrules.
agents.devinDesc: Install via Kiro adapter. Hooks load automatically from .kiro/hooks/.
agents.devinExample: bash install.sh --agent kiro — creates .kiro/hooks/ with pre-flight, commit approval, and edit guard hooks.
agents.anyAgentDesc: Copy the git hooks to your project. Works with any agent that uses git.
agents.principlesTitle: Using Principles in Your Own System
agents.principlesDesc: The core principles are agent-agnostic. Adopt them anywhere:
agents.principle1: TOOL_GAP — When tools can't verify, report "ship status unknown."
agents.principle2: Severity Labels — Classify findings: blocking → important → nit → suggestion → learning → praise.
agents.principle3: Error Path Design — Every tool call, gate, and loop needs a failure path.
agents.principle4: Continuation Over Recap — After context loss, resume. Don't waste tokens re-explaining.
agents.principle5: Drift Detection — Check docs vs reality regularly.
agents.principle6: Manifest Gate — Require a written summary before commit approval.

customization.title: Customization
customization.subtitle: How to extend the framework.
customization.editSkillsDesc: Skills are Markdown files in the skills/ directory. Edit them directly. Each skill has a SKILL.md index (≤250 lines) and optional guides in guides/.
customization.newSkillsDesc: Create a new directory in skills/ with a SKILL.md file. Include YAML frontmatter with name and description, a "When to Use" section, and keep it under 250 lines.
customization.addGuidesDesc: For detailed content that exceeds 250 lines, create guides in the guides/ subdirectory. Reference them from SKILL.md.
customization.configEnforcementDesc: Runtime hook controls via environment variables: SKILLS_HOOK_LEVEL, SKILLS_DISABLED_HOOKS, SKILLS_TEST_TIMEOUT.
customization.stackConfigDesc: Edit STACK_CONFIG.md in your project to customize commands (test, lint, build, dev). All skills read from this file.
customization.userProfileDesc: Your preferences are stored at ~/.config/opencode/user-profile.json. These persist across all projects.
customization.evalsDesc: Skills can verify their own output quality using evals.md files. Each skill defines pass/fail checks that the agent runs after execution. This is the practical implementation of the Mayéutic Challenge — analyze before executing.
customization.memoryDesc: Skills can maintain memory.md files that log lessons from past debugging sessions. This creates a learning loop where the skill improves over time. Entries are categorized as error, learning, or observation.

eval.title: Evaluation Guide
eval.subtitle: How the skill evaluation system verifies skill quality and reliability.
eval.whatIsDesc: The evaluation system verifies that every skill behaves correctly across four failure modes defined by the Agent Skills whitepaper (Singhal et al., 2026): trigger (does the skill activate when it should?), execution (is the output correct?), token budget (is it within limits?), and regression (do co-loaded skills interfere?).
eval.howToAddDesc: Each skill has an evals/ directory with up to three files: trigger.jsonl (positive and negative trigger tests), golden.jsonl (known-good inputs with rubrics), and adversarial.jsonl (rephrasing, boundary, and edge cases).
eval.runningDesc: Use the eval scripts to validate skill behavior across all 57 skills.
eval.tiersDesc: Every skill has a tier in its frontmatter: read-only (analyzes, never writes), draft (in development), action-allowed (full capability).
eval.fullDocDesc: For the complete reference including format specification, schema, trigger accuracy targets, coverage checklist, and examples, read EVAL-GUIDE.md.

designReview.title: Design Review Pipeline
designReview.subtitle: 9 skills. One pipeline. Deterministic quality.
designReview.critiqueDesc: Heuristic review using Nielsen's 10 usability heuristics and 4 persona tests. Detects AI slop patterns. Produces a score per heuristic.
designReview.auditDesc: 5-dimension scan: accessibility, performance, theming, responsive, anti-patterns. P0-P3 severity scoring.
designReview.clarifyDesc: Rewrites labels, errors, buttons, empty states, confirmations. Voice-tuned per audience.
designReview.hardDesc: Mechanical P0/P1 fixes: ARIA attributes, keyboard navigation, form validation, error/empty/loading states.
designReview.polishDesc: Fixes spacing, alignment, token drift, border radius, shadows. No design decisions — only compliance.
designReview.typesetDesc: Applies type ramp, fixes line-height, letter-spacing, paragraph rhythm.
designReview.adaptDesc: Breakpoints, touch targets (≥44px), viewport handling, mobile overflow.
designReview.optimizeDesc: Bundle size, lazy loading, image optimization, animation compositing, layout thrashing.
designReview.delightDesc: Hover/tap feedback, state transitions, loading animation, success/error feedback.

rules.title: Rules
rules.subtitle: Behavioral rules that govern agent conduct.
rules.rule0Desc: Before loading any skill, the agent checks ~/.config/opencode/user-profile.json for saved preferences (stack, purpose, testing framework). This avoids asking the same questions every session.
rules.rule0bDesc: When re-entering a project, check HEALTH-CHECK.md age. If < 7 days, resume from last state. If > 7 days, re-audit. Never start from zero.
rules.rule0cDesc: Four principles: (1) Think Before Coding — surface assumptions first. (2) Simplicity First — ask "what is the simplest thing that could work?" (3) Surgical Changes — touch only what the task requires. (4) Goal-Driven — every action serves the stated objective.
rules.rule0gDesc: Before executing non-trivial tasks, the agent must present: code affected, edge cases found, alternatives considered, risks identified. The user decides whether to proceed. Named after the Socratic method — questioning to reveal truth.
rules.rule0hDesc: When verification tools cannot reach the world (no network, no build tool, no test runner), the agent MUST report "ship status unknown" and STOP. Never infer success from absence of failure. The absence of evidence is not evidence of absence.
rules.rule0iDesc: After context loss (compaction, session restart), the agent should CONTINUE from the last verified state, not RECAP everything. Ask "Where were we?" instead of "Let me summarize." Recap wastes tokens. Continuation preserves momentum.
rules.rule0dDesc: Before ANY mutation: (1) pre-flight check git state, (2) branch interview — wrong branch? dirty tree?, (3) edit guard — structural integrity, (4) commit barrier — manifest + approval. This is a checklist, not a suggestion.
rules.rule12Desc: Before every mutation (commit, push, merge, rebase), the agent presents a DECISION POINT block with: files changed, impact, risk, and asks for approval. Invalid responses: "ok", "mmhm", silence. Plan approval and commit approval are ALWAYS separate decisions.
rules.rule12bDesc: Before merging any PR, run pr-review-checklist.sh. It checks 8 mechanical gates: tests pass, build works, no secrets, no slop, skill consulted, etc. No merge without passing all gates.
rules.rule0eDesc: When context reaches 70% usage, evict oldest non-active content. Compress conversation history. NEVER evict active work (current task, in-progress files). Auto-eviction prevents context overflow.
rules.rule0fDesc: Skills, rules, and hooks are loaded via native plugin mechanisms. OpenCode uses skill tool, Claude Code uses .claude-plugin/, Cursor uses .cursor-plugin/. Each agent has its own loading path.
rules.rule6Desc: Every skill is a ~250-line index (SKILL.md). Detailed content lives in guides/ subdirectories. The agent only loads what it needs. This saves 50% of always-loaded tokens.
rules.rule8Desc: Context budget: 60% for current task, 25% for conversation history, 15% for rules/skills. Auto-compaction triggers at >20 messages. Prevents context overflow.
rules.rule1Desc: Before implementing anything, the agent loads using-agent-skills to identify which skill applies. Skills encode processes that prevent common mistakes. Never implement directly if a skill exists for the task.
rules.rule2Desc: Before acting, detect the platform: Node, Rust, Python, Go, etc. Different platforms have different tools, patterns, and conventions. STACK_CONFIG.md provides the mapping.
rules.rule3Desc: Every task follows: DEFINE (spec) → PLAN (tasks) → BUILD (implement) → VERIFY (test) → REVIEW (check) → SHIP (deploy). No phase is skipped. Purpose-driven execution weights phases by session type.
rules.rule4Desc: Turbo Mode reduces scope, never discipline. It skips OPTIONAL phases (e.g., design review for simple bug fixes) but never skips MANDATORY phases (e.g., verification, commit approval).
rules.rule5Desc: Default stack detection from lockfiles (package.json, Cargo.toml, etc.). User can override via STACK_CONFIG.md. Skills read from STACK_CONFIG.md, not hardcoded commands.
rules.rule7Desc: No code is written until SPEC.md, DESIGN.md, and .gitignore exist. The spec defines WHAT to build. The design defines HOW it looks. The .gitignore defines what to exclude.
rules.rule9Desc: Before marking any task complete, run the verification checklist: tests pass, build works, no regressions, documentation updated. If any check fails, the task is NOT complete.
rules.rule10Desc: Never mix languages in a single file or component. TypeScript files contain TypeScript. Python files contain Python. Mixing creates maintenance nightmares and breaks tooling.
rules.rule11Desc: All development artifacts (SESSION_CONTEXT.md, INCIDENT docs, remediation plans) go in the development/ directory. This keeps the project root clean and .gitignore can exclude the entire directory.
